summary: Fournier argues that hackdays serve as an excellent metric for measuring the success of development platform migrations. Rent the Runway spent two years migrating from a complex Drupal monolith to Java services with a Ruby frontend, primarily to improve development velocity rather than just handle load. Their first post-migration hackday produced dramatically better results than previous ones—over ten production-worthy projects versus mostly non-functional simulations. The difference wasn't team effort or intelligence, but rather that the new platform enabled rather than resisted creative development.
key_points: Replatforming for development scalability is harder to measure than replatforming for load, but hackday output provides a concrete metric
The incremental 'hollowing out' approach—gradually replacing pieces while keeping the business running—proved effective for a major platform migration
A good platform enables developers to accomplish meaningful work without requiring complete system knowledge
The quality difference in hackday output directly reflects whether your architecture supports or fights against developer productivity
money_quotes: It's pretty common for teams, especially startups, to get to a point with their tech stack where the platform they've been working on just isn't cutting it anymore.
We wanted to burn the whole thing to the ground and start fresh.
We didn't do this, of course. We were running a successful business on that old hideous platform.
The team didn't work any less hard, nor was it any less smart than it is now. The major difference between that hack day and now is that we've replatformed our system.
Now adding new pages to the site is simple and doesn't require full knowledge of all the legacy Drupal.

You may be surprised what you learn, and you'll almost certainly be impressed with what your team can accomplish when given creative freedom and a platform that doesn't resist every attempt at creativity.
key_insight: A hackday's output serves as a concrete, measurable indicator of whether a platform migration has successfully improved development velocity—if the same team produces dramatically better results, the replatforming worked.
